questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: For a given body, the ratio of its specific heat capacity to its molar heat capacity:
options: is a dimensionless quantity
is a universal constant
is dependent on the mass of the body
is dependent on the molecular weight of the body
questions.solution:
Specific heat capacity $s$ is the amount of heat required to raise the temperature of unit mass of a substance by $1^{\circ}\text{C}$.

$s = \dfrac{\Delta Q}{m \Delta T}$

Molar heat capacity $C_m$ is the amount of heat required to raise the temperature of one mole of a substance by $1^{\circ}\text{C}$.

$C_m = \dfrac{\Delta Q}{n \Delta T}$

Taking the ratio of specific heat capacity to molar heat capacity:

$\dfrac{s}{C_m} = \dfrac{\dfrac{\Delta Q}{m \Delta T}}{\dfrac{\Delta Q}{n \Delta T}} = \dfrac{n}{m}$

The number of moles $n$ is given by $n = \dfrac{m}{M}$, where $m$ is the mass and $M$ is the molecular weight of the body.

$\dfrac{s}{C_m} = \dfrac{\dfrac{m}{M}}{m} = \dfrac{1}{M}$

Thus, the ratio of specific heat capacity to molar heat capacity is inversely proportional to the molecular weight of the body, meaning it is dependent on the molecular weight.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: When heat is provided to a solid, its temperature
options: may remain constant
may increase
may decrease
must increase
questions.solution:
When heat is supplied to a solid, it can be utilized in two ways.

If the solid is not at its melting point, the supplied heat increases the kinetic energy of its molecules, which results in an increase in its temperature.

If the solid is at its melting point, the supplied heat is used as latent heat to change its phase from solid to liquid. During this phase transition, the temperature of the solid remains constant.

Therefore, the temperature of the solid may increase or may remain constant.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The heat capacity of a body is dependent on
options: the increase in temperature
the mass of the body
the heat supplied
the material of the body
questions.solution:
Heat capacity of a body is defined as the amount of heat required to raise its temperature by unity.

$C = \dfrac{\Delta Q}{\Delta T} = m c$

where $m$ is the mass of the body and $c$ is the specific heat capacity of the material.

Since specific heat capacity is a characteristic property of the material, the heat capacity of a body depends on its mass and the material it is made of.

It is independent of the heat supplied and the increase in temperature.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The temperature of an object is seen to increase over a certain period. During this period,
options: work might have been done on it
heat might have been supplied to it
heat is definitely supplied to it
heat is definitely not supplied to it
questions.solution:
According to the first law of thermodynamics, $\Delta Q = \Delta U + \Delta W$, where $\Delta Q$ is the heat supplied to the system, $\Delta U$ is the change in internal energy, and $\Delta W$ is the work done by the system.

An increase in temperature indicates an increase in the internal energy of the object ($\Delta U > 0$).

From the first law, $\Delta U$ can be positive if heat is supplied to the object ($\Delta Q > 0$) or if work is done on the object ($\Delta W < 0$), or both.

Thus, work might have been done on the object, or heat might have been supplied to it. It is not necessary that heat is definitely supplied, as the temperature can increase adiabatically by doing work on the object alone.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: During a specific time interval, the temperature of a solid object is observed to remain constant. In this interval,
options: no heat is supplied to the object
heat may have been extracted from the object
heat may have been supplied to the object
no heat is extracted from the object
questions.solution:
During the time interval when the temperature of the solid object is constant, it is possible that the object is undergoing a phase change (such as melting or sublimation). During a phase change, heat can be supplied to or extracted from the object without any change in its temperature.

Alternatively, the object might be in thermal equilibrium with its surroundings, meaning the rate at which heat is supplied to it equals the rate at which heat is extracted from it. Thus, heat may have been supplied to or extracted from the object.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The statement that heat and work are equivalent implies that
options: a body initially at rest can be set into linear motion by providing heat to it
when heat is provided to a body, we perform work on it
when work is performed on a body, we provide heat to it
the temperature of a body can be raised by performing work on it
questions.solution:
The equivalence of heat and work implies that both are forms of energy transfer that can produce the same effect on a system.

According to Joule's principle of the mechanical equivalent of heat, mechanical work done on a system can increase its internal energy, which manifests as a rise in its temperature.

This produces the exact same effect as supplying heat to the system. Therefore, the temperature of a body can be raised by performing work on it.
